pathways.label: Adult arrest-record expungement under SDCL § 23A-3-27
pathways.summary: A person may apply to the court with jurisdiction over the crime for which the person was arrested if one of these applies: Arrest occurred, but no accusatory instrument was filed After 1 year from arrest Prosecutor formally dismissed the entire criminal case After 1 year from formal dismissal Person was acquitted Any time after acquittal Prosecutor formally dismissed the entire criminal case and there is compelling necessity Within 1 year from dismissal, if compelling necessity is shown

If your South Dakota case did not result in a conviction, you may have an expungement path. The timing depends on whether no charge was filed, the whole case was dismissed, or you were acquitted.

South Dakotas UJS instructions say the motion starts a new civil action, and it should be filed in the same court that has jurisdiction over the underlying case or arrest. The instructions also warn that the motion can only be made on a record of arrest, and more than one arrest record requires a separate case for each arrest record.

The current UJS instructions say the petitioner files the required motion and case filing statement with the clerk where the arrest or case is filed and pays a $72 filing fee. The statute separately says the fee is the same as the civil filing fee and may be waived if the court finds the applicant is indigent. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-28/")

The motion must be served on the prosecuting attorney at least 14 days before the hearing, and the prosecutor may contest the motion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-28/")

A hearing may be set unless the hearing is waived by the arrested person/defendant, the prosecutor, and any victim. The court may require affidavits, testimony, or other evidence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-29/")

The court may enter an expungement order only after finding by clear and convincing evidence that the ends of justice and the best interests of the public and the defendant or arrested person will be served. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-30/")
1 Confirm the correct court and county.
2 Confirm whether this is one arrest record or multiple arrest records.
3 Prepare UJS-391 Motion for Expungement and Statement of Mailing.
4 Prepare UJS-232 Case Filing Statement.
5 File with the clerk and pay the filing fee or request fee waiver/indigency relief.
6 Mail/serve the States Attorney.
7 If hearing is required, prepare UJS-393 Notice of Hearing and serve at least 14 days before hearing.
8 If all required parties waive hearing, prepare UJS-392 Waiver of Hearing.
9 Prepare proposed UJS-394 Order for Expungement.

South Dakota has a special diversion path. A person who successfully completes a diversion program may be eligible for expungement of the entire criminal record related to the arrest if they are not charged with a new crime, other than petty offenses and minor traffic violations, within one year and thirty days after successful completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-35/")

If you completed a South Dakota diversion program and stayed clear of new charges for one year and thirty days, the States Attorney should file the dismissal and diversion-completion notice, and the court should grant expungement without a separate motion.

South Dakota automatically removes certain minor cases from the public record after five years if all court-ordered conditions are satisfied and the person has no further convictions during that five-year period. This applies when the highest charged offense in the case was a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-34/")

Some low-level South Dakota cases are removed from public view after five years if the case was only a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor, all conditions were completed, and there were no new convictions. But the record may still be used by the court or to enhance later penalties.

South Dakota has a special deferred route for certain drug charges under SDCL 23A-27-53. For a guilty plea to specified controlled-substance offenses, the court may defer sentencing and require probation, treatment, testing, service, or other conditions. After one year, if the person completed treatment and all required conditions, the court must dismiss the original charge under the listed drug statutes when the person pleads guilty to the specified lesser offense. The statute also says the person is not eligible if there are aggravating circumstances or if the person is serving a sentence with the executive branch. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-53/")

A South Dakota pardon through Chapter 24-14 releases the person from penalties and disabilities resulting from the offense. When the Governor grants a pardon under that chapter, the Governor orders official records relating to the arrest, charging document, trial, finding of guilt, pardon application, and Board of Pardons and Paroles proceedings sealed. A public document certifying the pardon remains filed with the Secretary of State for five years, then is sealed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")

A juvenile, parent, guardian, or guardian ad litem may petition to seal juvenile delinquency records. The petition may be filed only after one year has passed from the juveniles unconditional release from court jurisdiction or Department of Corrections discharge, whichever is later. The court may seal records if the child has not been adjudicated delinquent since release/discharge, has no pending or instituted serious proceeding of the types listed in the statute, and the court finds rehabilitation has been attained. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-26/chapter-07a/section-26-7a-115/")

pathways.label: Suspended-imposition-of-sentence sealing
pathways.summary: South Dakotas suspended imposition of sentence, often called SIS, is separate from ordinary arrest expungement. For certain felonies not punishable by death or life imprisonment, a court may suspend imposition of sentence if the person has never before been convicted of a felony. A person may not receive a second felony SIS if they already had one. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-13/") After the person completes all court conditions, the defendant is discharged and the case is dismissed without adjudication of guilt. The discharge and dismissal are not deemed a conviction for disqualifications…
ruleClauses: South Dakotas suspended imposition of sentence, often called SIS, is separate from ordinary arrest expungement. For certain felonies not punishable by death or life imprisonment, a court may suspend imposition of sentence if the person has never before been convicted of a felony. A person may not receive a second felony SIS if they already had one. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-13/")

After the person completes all court conditions, the defendant is discharged and the case is dismissed without adjudication of guilt. The discharge and dismissal are not deemed a conviction for disqualifications or disabilities imposed by law. South Dakota limits the benefit to once for felony SIS and once for misdemeanor SIS. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-14/")

The discharge/dismissal is reported to DCI, and a nonpublic disposition is retained for law enforcement, prosecutors, and courts for future sentencing, future SIS eligibility, and habitual-offender determinations. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-16/")

Upon discharge and dismissal, the court must seal all official records, other than the nonpublic DCI records. The person is restored to the status before arrest, indictment, or information and is not guilty of perjury or false statement for failing to acknowledge the case. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-17/")

South Dakota has a special diversion path. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-35/")

If you completed a South Dakota diversion program and stayed clear of new charges for one year and thirty days, the States Attorney should file the dismissal and diversion-completion notice, and the court should grant expungement without a separate motion.

South Dakota automatically removes certain minor cases from the public record after five years if all court-ordered conditions are satisfied and the person has no further convictions during that five-year period. This applies when the highest charged offense in the case was a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-34/")

Some low-level South Dakota cases are removed from public view after five years if the case was only a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor, all conditions were completed, and there were no new convictions. But the record may still be used by the court or to enhance later penalties.

For a guilty plea to specified controlled-substance offenses, the court may defer sentencing and require probation, treatment, testing, service, or other conditions. The statute also says the person is not eligible if there are aggravating circumstances or if the person is serving a sentence with the executive branch. A South Dakota pardon through Chapter 24-14 releases the person from penalties and disabilities resulting from the offense. When the Governor grants a pardon under that chapter, the Governor orders official records relating to the arrest, charging document, trial, finding of guilt, pardon application, and Board of Pardons and Paroles proceedings sealed. A public document certifying the pardon remains filed with the Secretary of State for five years, then is sealed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")

A juvenile, parent, guardian, or guardian ad litem may petition to seal juvenile delinquency records. The petition may be filed only after one year has passed from the juveniles unconditional release from court jurisdiction or Department of Corrections discharge, whichever is later. The court may seal records if the child has not been adjudicated delinquent since release/discharge, has no pending or instituted serious proceeding of the types listed in the statute, and the court finds rehabilitation has been attained. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-26/chapter-07a/section-26-7a-115/")

ruleClauses: A South Dakota pardon through Chapter 24-14 releases the person from penalties and disabilities resulting from the offense. When the Governor grants a pardon under that chapter, the Governor orders official records relating to the arrest, charging document, trial, finding of guilt, pardon application, and Board of Pardons and Paroles proceedings sealed. A public document certifying the pardon remains filed with the Secretary of State for five years, then is sealed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")

A pardon still may count as a prior conviction for later sentencing, habitual-offender proceedings, and later DUI sentencing. DCI retains a nonpublic record for use by law enforcement, prosecutors, and courts. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")

The South Dakota Department of Corrections explains that only the Governor can grant a pardon, the Board of Pardons and Paroles reviews applications and makes recommendations, and South Dakota law does not allow sealing unless the pardon goes through the Chapter 24-14 Board process. ("https://docpardon.sd.gov/faq.aspx")

South Dakota has a special diversion path. A person who successfully completes a diversion program may be eligible for expungement of the entire criminal record related to the arrest if they are not charged with a new crime, other than petty offenses and minor traffic violations, within one year and thirty days after successful completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-35/")

If you completed a South Dakota diversion program and stayed clear of new charges for one year and thirty days, the States Attorney should file the dismissal and diversion-completion notice, and the court should grant expungement without a separate motion.

South Dakota automatically removes certain minor cases from the public record after five years if all court-ordered conditions are satisfied and the person has no further convictions during that five-year period. This applies when the highest charged offense in the case was a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-34/")

Some low-level South Dakota cases are removed from public view after five years if the case was only a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor, all conditions were completed, and there were no new convictions. But the record may still be used by the court or to enhance later penalties.

South Dakota has a special deferred route for certain drug charges under SDCL 23A-27-53. For a guilty plea to specified controlled-substance offenses, the court may defer sentencing and require probation, treatment, testing, service, or other conditions. After one year, if the person completed treatment and all required conditions, the court must dismiss the original charge under the listed drug statutes when the person pleads guilty to the specified lesser offense. The statute also says the person is not eligible if there are aggravating circumstances or if the person is serving a sentence with the executive branch. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-53/")

A South Dakota pardon through Chapter 24-14 releases the person from penalties and disabilities resulting from the offense. When the Governor grants a pardon under that chapter, the Governor orders official records relating to the arrest, charging document, trial, finding of guilt, pardon application, and Board of Pardons and Paroles proceedings sealed. A public document certifying the pardon remains filed with the Secretary of State for five years, then is sealed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")

A juvenile, parent, guardian, or guardian ad litem may petition to seal juvenile delinquency records. The petition may be filed only after one year has passed from the juveniles unconditional release from court jurisdiction or Department of Corrections discharge, whichever is later. The court may seal records if the child has not been adjudicated delinquent since release/discharge, has no pending or instituted serious proceeding of the types listed in the statute, and the court finds rehabilitation has been attained. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-26/chapter-07a/section-26-7a-115/")

Your conviction is automatically erased.
Automatic removal of a Class 2 misdemeanor means the record can never be used again.

South Dakota defines expungement as the sealing of criminal records in courts, detention/correctional facilities, law enforcement agencies, criminal justice agencies, and the Department of Public Safety. The statute specifically says expungement does not imply physical destruction of records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-26/")
In South Dakota, expungement usually means the record is sealed from public access. It does not necessarily mean the record is destroyed.

South Dakotas general expungement process is mainly tied to arrest records where no charging document was filed, the case was dismissed, or the person was acquitted. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-27/")

filingDestinationRules: South Dakotas UJS instructions say the motion starts a new civil action, and it should be filed in the same court that has jurisdiction over the underlying case or arrest. The instructions also warn that the motion can only be made on a record of arrest, and more than one arrest record requires a separate case for each arrest record.

The current UJS instructions say the petitioner files the required motion and case filing statement with the clerk where the arrest or case is filed and pays a $72 filing fee. The statute separately says the fee is the same as the civil filing fee and may be waived if the court finds the applicant is indigent. sourceSections.title: Core South Dakota rule
sourceSections.text:
South Dakota defines expungement as the sealing of criminal records in courts, detention/correctional facilities, law enforcement agencies, criminal justice agencies, and the Department of Public Safety. The statute specifically says expungement does not imply physical destruction of records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-26/")
Wilma plain-language script:
In South Dakota, expungement usually means the record is sealed from public access. It does not necessarily mean the record is destroyed.

South Dakotas general expungement process is mainly tied to arrest records where no charging document was filed, the case was dismissed, or the person was acquitted. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-27/")

sourceSections.title: Main South Dakota pathways
sourceSections.text:
Pathway Main authority Best used for
Arrest record expungement SDCL 23A-3-26 to 23A-3-32 No accusatory instrument, dismissal, acquittal
Diversion expungement SDCL 23A-3-35 to 23A-3-37 Successful diversion completion
Automatic public-record removal for minor cases SDCL 23A-3-34 Petty offense, municipal ordinance, or Class 2 misdemeanor highest charge
Suspended imposition sealing SDCL 23A-27-13 to 23A-27-17 Successful suspended imposition of sentence
Drug deferred/special dismissal route SDCL 23A-27-53 Certain controlled-substance cases after treatment/compliance
Pardon sealing SDCL 24-14-11 Governors pardon through Chapter 24-14 process
Juvenile delinquency sealing SDCL 26-7A-115 to 26-7A-116 Juvenile records after statutory waiting and rehabilitation showing
Juvenile trafficking expungement SDCL 26-7A-115.1 Delinquency records caused by trafficking or sexual exploitation

sourceSections.title: Adult arrest expungement: SDCL 23A-3-27
sourceSections.text:
A person may apply to the court with jurisdiction over the crime for which the person was arrested if one of these applies:

Situation Timing
Arrest occurred, but no accusatory instrument was filed After 1 year from arrest
Prosecutor formally dismissed the entire criminal case After 1 year from formal dismissal
Person was acquitted Any time after acquittal
Prosecutor formally dismissed the entire criminal case and there is compelling necessity Within 1 year from dismissal, if compelling necessity is shown
This is the core adult South Dakota expungement screen. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-27/")
Wilma status labels:
likely_eligible_no_accusatory_instrument_1_year
likely_eligible_formal_dismissal_1_year
likely_eligible_acquittal_anytime
possible_compelling_necessity_dismissal_within_1_year
not_eligible_yet_waiting_period
needs_disposition_or_dismissal_order
Wilma plain-language script:
If your South Dakota case did not result in a conviction, you may have an expungement path. The timing depends on whether no charge was filed, the whole case was dismissed, or you were acquitted.

sourceSections.title: Filing process for adult arrest expungement
sourceSections.text:
South Dakotas UJS instructions say the motion starts a new civil action, and it should be filed in the same court that has jurisdiction over the underlying case or arrest. The instructions also warn that the motion can only be made on a record of arrest, and more than one arrest record requires a separate case for each arrest record.
The current UJS instructions say the petitioner files the required motion and case filing statement with the clerk where the arrest or case is filed and pays a $72 filing fee. The statute separately says the fee is the same as the civil filing fee and may be waived if the court finds the applicant is indigent. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-28/")
The motion must be served on the prosecuting attorney at least 14 days before the hearing, and the prosecutor may contest the motion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-28/")
A hearing may be set unless the hearing is waived by the arrested person/defendant, the prosecutor, and any victim. The court may require affidavits, testimony, or other evidence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-29/")
The court may enter an expungement order only after finding by clear and convincing evidence that the ends of justice and the best interests of the public and the defendant or arrested person will be served. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-30/")
Wilma filing checklist:
1 Confirm the correct court and county.
2 Confirm whether this is one arrest record or multiple arrest records.
3 Prepare UJS-391 Motion for Expungement and Statement of Mailing.
4 Prepare UJS-232 Case Filing Statement.
5 File with the clerk and pay the filing fee or request fee waiver/indigency relief.
6 Mail/serve the States Attorney.
7 If hearing is required, prepare UJS-393 Notice of Hearing and serve at least 14 days before hearing.
8 If all required parties waive hearing, prepare UJS-392 Waiver of Hearing.
9 Prepare proposed UJS-394 Order for Expungement.
10 After order is signed, prepare/file UJS-395 Notice of Entry of Order and mail it with the order to the States Attorney.

sourceSections.title: Effect of adult arrest expungement
sourceSections.text:
After expungement, the court reports the order to the Division of Criminal Investigation. South Dakota preserves a nonpublic disposition record for use only by law enforcement, prosecutors, and courts for sentencing on later offenses. The court orders official records sealed other than those retained nonpublic DCI records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-31/")
The effect is to restore the person to the status occupied before the arrest, indictment, or information. The person is not guilty of perjury or making a false statement for failing to disclose the arrest, indictment, information, or trial. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-32/")
Wilma plain-language script:
If the court grants expungement, the public record is sealed and you may generally answer as if the arrest or case did not happen. But South Dakota still allows certain nonpublic records to be retained for law enforcement, prosecutor, and court use in later sentencing.

sourceSections.title: Diversion expungement
sourceSections.text:
South Dakota has a special diversion path. A person who successfully completes a diversion program may be eligible for expungement of the entire criminal record related to the arrest if they are not charged with a new crime, other than petty offenses and minor traffic violations, within one year and thirty days after successful completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-35/")
If the statutory requirements are met, the States Attorney must file a dismissal of all charges related to the arrest and a notice that the person completed diversion. After that filing, the court must grant expungement without a motion or further court action. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-36/") ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-37/")
Wilma status labels:
likely_eligible_diversion_auto_expungement
not_eligible_yet_diversion_wait_1_year_30_days
new_charge_during_diversion_wait_review
needs_diversion_completion_notice
Wilma script:
If you completed a South Dakota diversion program and stayed clear of new charges for one year and thirty days, the States Attorney should file the dismissal and diversion-completion notice, and the court should grant expungement without a separate motion.

sourceSections.title: Automatic public-record removal for petty, municipal, and Class 2 misdemeanor cases
sourceSections.text:
South Dakota automatically removes certain minor cases from the public record after five years if all court-ordered conditions are satisfied and the person has no further convictions during that five-year period. This applies when the highest charged offense in the case was a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-34/")
The record is not gone for every purpose. It remains available to court personnel or by court order and may be used to enhance a penalty for a later criminal offense. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-03/section-23a-3-34/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_auto_public_record_removal_minor_offense_5_years
not_eligible_minor_auto_removal_conditions_unpaid
not_eligible_minor_auto_removal_new_conviction
record_may_still_enhance_later_offense
Wilma script:
Some low-level South Dakota cases are removed from public view after five years if the case was only a petty offense, municipal ordinance violation, or Class 2 misdemeanor, all conditions were completed, and there were no new convictions. But the record may still be used by the court or to enhance later penalties.

sourceSections.title: Suspended imposition of sentence sealing
sourceSections.text:
South Dakotas suspended imposition of sentence, often called SIS, is separate from ordinary arrest expungement. For certain felonies not punishable by death or life imprisonment, a court may suspend imposition of sentence if the person has never before been convicted of a felony. A person may not receive a second felony SIS if they already had one. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-13/")
After the person completes all court conditions, the defendant is discharged and the case is dismissed without adjudication of guilt. The discharge and dismissal are not deemed a conviction for disqualifications or disabilities imposed by law. South Dakota limits the benefit to once for felony SIS and once for misdemeanor SIS. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-14/")
The discharge/dismissal is reported to DCI, and a nonpublic disposition is retained for law enforcement, prosecutors, and courts for future sentencing, future SIS eligibility, and habitual-offender determinations. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-16/")
Upon discharge and dismissal, the court must seal all official records, other than the nonpublic DCI records. The person is restored to the status before arrest, indictment, or information and is not guilty of perjury or false statement for failing to acknowledge the case. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-17/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_sis_discharge_and_sealing
possible_felony_sis_23a_27_13
not_eligible_prior_felony_for_felony_sis
not_eligible_prior_felony_sis
needs_sis_discharge_order
dci_nonpublic_record_retained
Wilma script:
If you received a suspended imposition of sentence and completed all conditions, South Dakota may dismiss and seal the case. That is different from a standard arrest expungement, and DCI may still retain a nonpublic record for future court and law-enforcement purposes.

sourceSections.title: Special controlled-substance deferred route
sourceSections.text:
South Dakota has a special deferred route for certain drug charges under SDCL 23A-27-53. For a guilty plea to specified controlled-substance offenses, the court may defer sentencing and require probation, treatment, testing, service, or other conditions. After one year, if the person completed treatment and all required conditions, the court must dismiss the original charge under the listed drug statutes when the person pleads guilty to the specified lesser offense. The statute also says the person is not eligible if there are aggravating circumstances or if the person is serving a sentence with the executive branch. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-23a/chapter-27/section-23a-27-53/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_drug_deferred_imposition_23a_27_53
needs_treatment_completion_proof
needs_legal_review_drug_deferred_route
not_eligible_aggravating_circumstances
Wilma script:
South Dakota has a special drug-case deferred route that can lead to dismissal of the original charge after treatment and compliance. This should be reviewed carefully because it depends on the exact statute, plea, treatment completion, and whether aggravating circumstances exist.

sourceSections.title: Pardon-based sealing
sourceSections.text:
A South Dakota pardon through Chapter 24-14 releases the person from penalties and disabilities resulting from the offense. When the Governor grants a pardon under that chapter, the Governor orders official records relating to the arrest, charging document, trial, finding of guilt, pardon application, and Board of Pardons and Paroles proceedings sealed. A public document certifying the pardon remains filed with the Secretary of State for five years, then is sealed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")
A pardon still may count as a prior conviction for later sentencing, habitual-offender proceedings, and later DUI sentencing. DCI retains a nonpublic record for use by law enforcement, prosecutors, and courts. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-24/chapter-14/section-24-14-11/")
The South Dakota Department of Corrections explains that only the Governor can grant a pardon, the Board of Pardons and Paroles reviews applications and makes recommendations, and South Dakota law does not allow sealing unless the pardon goes through the Chapter 24-14 Board process. ("https://docpardon.sd.gov/faq.aspx")
Wilma status labels:
possible_pardon_sealing_chapter_24_14
pardon_not_same_as_expungement
pardon_still_counts_for_later_sentencing
needs_board_process_for_sealing
Wilma script:
A South Dakota pardon can lead to sealing, but only if it goes through the Chapter 24-14 pardon process. A pardon does not erase the case for every purpose, and it can still count as a prior conviction in later sentencing situations.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile delinquency sealing
sourceSections.text:
A juvenile, parent, guardian, or guardian ad litem may petition to seal juvenile delinquency records. The petition may be filed only after one year has passed from the juveniles unconditional release from court jurisdiction or Department of Corrections discharge, whichever is later. The court may seal records if the child has not been adjudicated delinquent since release/discharge, has no pending or instituted serious proceeding of the types listed in the statute, and the court finds rehabilitation has been attained. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-26/chapter-07a/section-26-7a-115/")
After sealing, later inspection is limited, but the record can still be used in certain juvenile proceedings or later criminal sentencing. The custodian of sealed records may inspect or access sealed records in the performance of official duties. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-26/chapter-07a/section-26-7a-116/")
Wilma status labels:

sourceSections.title: Juvenile trafficking expungement
sourceSections.text:
A child who was a victim of human trafficking or sexual exploitation may petition, directly or through a parent, guardian, or guardian ad litem, for expungement of a delinquency record that resulted from trafficking or sexual exploitation. Expungement under this statute vacates the underlying delinquency proceeding. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-dakota/title-26/chapter-07a/section-26-7a-115-1/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_juvenile_trafficking_expungement
legal_aid_handoff_juvenile_trafficking
delinquency_vacatur_possible
Wilma script:
If the juvenile record happened because the child was trafficked or sexually exploited, South Dakota has a special expungement route that may vacate the underlying delinquency proceeding. This should be routed to legal aid or an attorney.

sourceSections.title: Attorney/legal-aid referral triggers
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend legal review if:
The user is trying to expunge an adult conviction that is not clearly SIS, pardon-based, diversion-based, or minor-case automatic removal.
Any felony is involved.
Any DUI, domestic violence, sex offense, child-victim offense, violence, firearm, or protection-order issue is involved.
The person wants firearm-rights advice, immigration advice, licensing advice, or employment-disclosure advice.
The person wants to seek a pardon.
The person wants early expungement within one year of dismissal based on compelling necessity.
A victim may object or needs to waive hearing.
The case involves federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state records.
The person has multiple arrests in multiple counties.
The record is juvenile and involves trafficking, sexual exploitation, or later serious proceedings.
